Bitcoin as a decentralized digital curre.canonical: Bitcoin as a decentralized digital currency without intermediaries
Bitcoin as a decentralized digital curre.summary: Bitcoin is a decentralized digital currency introduced in 2009 that uses a peer-to-peer network and cryptography to let users transfer value without a central bank or traditional payment intermediary. Its transaction validation and issuance are handled collectively by the network rather than a single institution, and U.S. authorities have noted that it is not backed by any government or central bank and is not legal tender.
keyPoints: Satoshi Nakamoto’s white paper describes Bitcoin as a 'peer-to-peer electronic cash system,' designed to enable online payments without relying on a trusted third party.
Bitcoin uses peer-to-peer technology and a distributed ledger (blockchain), so transaction verification is performed by network participants instead of banks or a central operator.
The U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau states that virtual currencies such as Bitcoin are not backed by any government or central bank.

The Federal Reserve has explained that Bitcoin gave shape to the vision of a decentralized digital currency, but unlike U.S. currency it is not legal tender and is not a liability of the Federal Reserve or a bank.

Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash.canonical: Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System
Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash.summary: "Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System" is the original Bitcoin whitepaper by Satoshi Nakamoto, published on October 31, 2008. It proposes a decentralized electronic payment system that allows online payments to be sent directly between parties without a financial intermediary, using a peer-to-peer network, a chain of hash-based proof-of-work, and public-key signatures to address double-spending without relying on a trusted third party.
keyPoints: The paper was published on October 31, 2008, and is hosted by both Bitcoin.org and the Satoshi Nakamoto Institute as the original Bitcoin whitepaper.

Its core innovation is a peer-to-peer distributed timestamp server: transactions are grouped into blocks, hashed into a chain, and secured so history cannot be changed without redoing the accumulated proof-of-work.

The whitepaper specifies proof-of-work using SHA-256-style hashing, where miners search for a nonce producing a hash with the required zero bits; the valid chain is the one with the greatest cumulative proof-of-work.
The paper also introduces Simplified Payment Verification (SPV), describing how users can verify payments without running a full network node by relying on block headers and Merkle proofs.

Bitcoin creation in 2008 by Satoshi Naka.canonical: Bitcoin creation in 2008 by Satoshi Nakamoto
Bitcoin creation in 2008 by Satoshi Naka.summary: Search results confirm that Bitcoin’s concept was introduced in 2008 by the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto through the white paper "Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System." The project’s public footprint began in 2008 with the registration of bitcoin.org on August 18, 2008, while the white paper was released on October 31, 2008; the live network and software launch followed in January 2009.
keyPoints: Bitcoin.org states that Satoshi Nakamoto’s original paper is the document that first introduced Bitcoin, confirming Nakamoto as the author of the foundational white paper.
Bitcoin.org’s 10-year anniversary post says the bitcoin.org domain was registered on August 18, 2008, and was originally owned by Satoshi Nakamoto and Martti Malmi.
History.com reports that Nakamoto released the Bitcoin white paper on October 31, 2008, presenting Bitcoin as a peer-to-peer electronic cash system.
Britannica notes that the 2008 paper introduced cryptocurrency to a wider audience, while the actual Bitcoin software and network were launched in 2009.

Satoshi Nakamoto.summary: Satoshi Nakamoto is the pseudonym used by the unknown person or group that created Bitcoin. Search results consistently link the name to the 2008 Bitcoin white paper and the first Bitcoin software release, while reporting that Nakamoto’s real identity remains unverified despite years of speculation and failed claims.
keyPoints: Bitcoin.org hosts the original 2008 white paper, "Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System," credited to Satoshi Nakamoto.
Major reporting and reference sources describe "Satoshi Nakamoto" as a pseudonym rather than a confirmed real-world identity, and no person has been conclusively proven to be Bitcoin’s creator.
A UK High Court ruling in March 2024 found that Craig Wright was not Satoshi Nakamoto, rejecting one of the highest-profile public identity claims.
Historical coverage notes that Nakamoto released the Bitcoin protocol/software and later withdrew from public communication, leaving the project to other developers.

Bitcoin vs Visa transaction throughput.summary: Search results support the general comparison that Bitcoin’s base-layer throughput is on the order of single-digit transactions per second, while Visa’s network capacity is orders of magnitude higher. Visa materials explicitly state Bitcoin handles around 7 TPS, and Visa states VisaNet can process more than 65,000 transaction messages per second, with newer Visa pages citing 76,000 or even up to 83,000 messages per second. So the common claim “Bitcoin ~7 TPS vs Visa ~24,000 TPS” is directionally correct, but the Visa figure is not stable across sources and is often stated today as higher network-message capacity rather than 24,000 settled payment transactions per second.
keyPoints: Visa’s own crypto research page says: “Bitcoin manages around 7 TPS,” using Bitcoin as a low-throughput comparison point against other payment systems.

Bitcoin Core documentation exposes a `getchaintxstats` RPC that reports `txrate`, i.e. the measured average transactions-per-second over a chosen on-chain window, which indicates throughput is an empirical chain statistic rather than a fixed protocol constant.

Visa sources commonly describe VisaNet capacity as more than 65,000 transaction messages per second; a U.S. Visa merchant page cites more than 76,000 messages per second, and a 2025 Visa corporate article says up to 83,000 messages per second worldwide.
Because Visa often reports transaction-message capacity, while Bitcoin comparisons usually refer to confirmed on-chain transactions, the numbers are not perfectly apples-to-apples.

Bitcoin block reward schedule.summary: Bitcoin’s block reward schedule is a fixed issuance rule built into the Bitcoin protocol: the block subsidy began at 50 BTC per block in 2009 and is reduced by 50% every 210,000 blocks, which is approximately every four years. This declining subsidy is the main mechanism behind Bitcoin’s capped supply of roughly 21 million BTC, with issuance expected to taper toward zero around 2140. The most recent halving occurred at block 840,000 in April 2024, reducing the subsidy to 3.125 BTC per block.
keyPoints: Bitcoin launched with a 50 BTC block subsidy, and the subsidy halves every 210,000 blocks rather than on fixed calendar dates.
Past halving milestones occurred at block 210,000 in November 2012, block 420,000 in July 2016, block 630,000 in May 2020, and block 840,000 in April 2024.
Because Bitcoin targets an average block interval of about 10 minutes, 210,000 blocks works out to roughly four years, though exact halving dates vary.
The halving schedule causes total issuance to converge to about 21,000,000 BTC, with the subsidy expected to become negligible and effectively end around 2140.

Proof-of-Work mining process.summary: In Bitcoin’s proof-of-work mining process, miners assemble a candidate block and repeatedly vary fields such as the nonce to compute block-header hashes until one hash is numerically below the network’s current target. This is expensive to produce but easy for other nodes to verify, and Bitcoin adjusts the target every 2,016 blocks to keep block production near one block every 10 minutes.
keyPoints: A valid Bitcoin block must hash to a value below the current target; miners typically change the 32-bit nonce and other mutable block data to try new hashes.
Bitcoin uses the Hashcash-style proof-of-work system based on SHA-256 hashing, making block discovery a probabilistic trial-and-error process rather than solving a traditional algebraic puzzle.
Network difficulty is a measure of how hard it is to find a hash below the target, and Bitcoin retargets difficulty every 2,016 blocks.
Because each block includes the previous block’s hash, changing past data would require redoing the proof of work for that block and all of its successors.

Ethereum proposal and launch timeline.summary: Ethereum was conceived by Vitalik Buterin in late 2013, when he drafted the initial whitepaper outlining a generalized blockchain for smart contracts and decentralized applications. The project was announced publicly in January 2014, funded through an ether sale in July–August 2014, and officially launched on July 30, 2015 with the Genesis block and the Frontier release.
keyPoints: Vitalik Buterin said he wrote the initial draft of the Ethereum whitepaper in November 2013, and ethereum.org states the introductory paper was published in 2013 before launch in 2015.
Ethereum was presented publicly in January 2014; Buterin's "Ethereum: Now Going Public" post on January 23, 2014 described the project and its goals as a next-generation smart-contract platform.
To fund development, the founding team ran a public ether sale in July–August 2014, raising about 31,000 BTC, according to ethereum.org's history page.
Ethereum officially launched on July 30, 2015, when the Genesis block was mined and Frontier, the first live release of the network, went live.

Smart contract.summary: A smart contract is a blockchain-deployed program made up of code and data that executes on network nodes and records the results on-chain. In practice, smart contracts are used to automatically enforce predefined rules or conditions—often using simple if/then logic—so transactions or workflow steps can occur without relying on a central intermediary. On platforms such as Ethereum, they run exactly as programmed, and interactions are generally irreversible once executed.
keyPoints: NIST defines a smart contract as a collection of code and data deployed via cryptographically signed blockchain transactions; nodes execute it and must derive the same result, which is then recorded on the blockchain.

Ethereum’s documentation describes smart contracts as programs on the Ethereum blockchain that can define rules and automatically enforce them via code; they reside at a specific blockchain address and are triggered by transactions.

IBM explains that smart contracts typically execute when predefined conditions are met, often following if/then rules, and can automate actions such as releasing funds, issuing tickets, or advancing a workflow.

Because blockchain execution must be deterministic across nodes, smart contracts are designed so the same input produces the same output; external data sources used by contracts are commonly referred to as oracles.

Ethereum energy consumption reduced by a.canonical: Ethereum energy consumption reduced by approximately 99.95%
Ethereum energy consumption reduced by a.summary: Official Ethereum documentation states that The Merge—Ethereum’s transition from proof-of-work to proof-of-stake completed on September 15, 2022—reduced the network’s energy consumption by about 99.95%. The claim is widely repeated in Ethereum roadmap materials and is consistent with later analyses and academic studies that estimate roughly 99.95% to 99.98% lower energy use after the shift to proof-of-stake.
keyPoints: Ethereum.org’s The Merge and roadmap pages explicitly say the upgrade reduced Ethereum’s energy consumption by approximately 99.95%.
The Merge was the transition from proof-of-work to proof-of-stake, completed on September 15, 2022, replacing mining-based consensus with validator staking.
Ethereum.org’s energy-consumption page says its post-Merge estimates are based on a Crypto Carbon Ratings Institute study, providing an official methodological basis for the lower energy-use claim.
Independent research broadly supports the magnitude of the reduction: academic studies found post-Merge energy consumption fell by about 99.98% or 99.95%, depending on methodology.

Ethereum's transition from Proof of Work.canonical: Ethereum's transition from Proof of Work to Proof of Stake
Ethereum's transition from Proof of Work.summary: The Merge was Ethereum’s network upgrade that changed its consensus mechanism from Proof of Work to Proof of Stake on September 15, 2022. It combined Ethereum’s original mainnet with the Beacon Chain, a separate Proof of Stake chain launched earlier, ending ETH mining and replacing miners with validators who stake ETH to help secure the network. Official Ethereum documentation says the transition reduced Ethereum’s energy use by about 99.95%.
keyPoints: The Merge was executed on September 15, 2022, when Ethereum mainnet merged with the Beacon Chain and completed the move from PoW to PoS.
Ethereum’s Beacon Chain, the PoS consensus layer that later merged with mainnet, launched on December 1, 2020.
After the transition, Ethereum no longer relied on mining; block production and network security shifted to validators operating under the Proof of Stake system.
Ethereum.org reports that the move to Proof of Stake reduced the network’s energy consumption by roughly 99.95%.

DeFi TVL peak and decline in 2021.canonical: DeFi TVL peak and decline in 2021
DeFi TVL peak and decline in 2021.summary: Search results support the concept that DeFi total value locked (TVL) reached an all-time high in late 2021—commonly cited around $180 billion to $189 billion in November 2021, though some sources report higher figures depending on methodology and whether categories such as liquid staking or broader protocol sets are included. Multiple sources also confirm a sharp subsequent decline through 2022 and 2023, with TVL falling roughly 75% to 80% from that late-2021 peak amid the broader crypto bear market and major market shocks such as the Terra/Luna collapse.
keyPoints: Cointelegraph, citing DefiLlama, reported DeFi TVL hit a record of about $189 billion in November 2021 and later noted an approximately 80% drop to about $37 billion by October 2023.
A Queen's University economics working paper states DeFi TVL peaked around $180 billion in late 2021, aligning with the query's framing.

Some reports and papers cite higher late-2021 peaks—such as about $200 billion, $228 billion, or even $260 billion—showing that TVL peak estimates vary by data source, chain coverage, and inclusion criteria.

Regulatory and academic sources also describe a severe contraction after the peak, with one source noting DeFi TVL fell to roughly $80 billion by January 2023 and others highlighting the impact of the May 2022 Terra/Luna collapse.

Uniswap pioneered the automated market m.canonical: Uniswap pioneered the automated market maker model and launched in 2018
Uniswap pioneered the automated market m.summary: Search results support the concept. Uniswap v1 launched on Ethereum in November 2018, and multiple sources describe it as pioneering the automated market maker (AMM) approach for decentralized token trading. Uniswap replaced order books with smart-contract liquidity pools and the constant-product formula, helping establish the AMM model as a core design for DeFi exchanges.
keyPoints: Uniswap v1 launched on Ethereum mainnet in November 2018; one source specifies November 2, 2018 as the v1 launch date.
Uniswap’s own educational material says the Uniswap Protocol pioneered the AMM model for decentralized exchanges.
Instead of a traditional order book, Uniswap used liquidity pools and a constant-product pricing formula (x * y = k) to price trades automatically.
Later coverage describes Uniswap v1 as a proof of concept that helped popularize AMM-based decentralized exchanges and influenced the broader DeFi ecosystem.

Uniswap has processed over $1.8 trillion.canonical: Uniswap has processed over $1.8 trillion in trading volume since launch
Uniswap has processed over $1.8 trillion.summary: The claim is directionally correct but outdated. Official Uniswap materials show the protocol has processed far more than $1.8 trillion in cumulative trading volume since launch: Uniswap Labs says it has processed over $3 trillion to date, while an earlier official blog post said it had already reached $1.2 trillion by early 2023. Additional official and regulatory-source materials place cumulative volume above $1.5 trillion by 2023, indicating that 'over $1.8 trillion since launch' was plausible at some point but understates Uniswap’s current lifetime volume.
keyPoints: Uniswap Labs’ official 'About' page says the Uniswap Protocol has processed over $3 trillion in trading volume to date.
An official Uniswap blog post from early 2023 stated the protocol had supported $1.2 trillion in trading volume by then, showing rapid growth before later milestones.
A 2023 SEC comment letter referencing the protocol said cumulative volumes in 2023 exceeded $1.5 trillion across 6.5 million wallet addresses.
Uniswap governance reporting for April 2024 cited $77.48 billion in monthly protocol volume, consistent with continued growth beyond the $1.8 trillion lifetime threshold.

Automated market maker model.summary: An automated market maker (AMM) is a decentralized exchange model that replaces order books with smart-contract liquidity pools. In the standard AMM setup, liquidity providers deposit pairs of tokens—typically at equal value—into a pool, and traders swap against that pooled liquidity while prices are determined algorithmically from the pool’s asset balances.
keyPoints: Chainlink defines a liquidity pool as a smart contract holding two or more tokens for decentralized trading, and notes that a typical LP deposits an equal value of two different tokens such as ETH and USDC.

Coinbase explains that AMMs use liquidity pools plus a constant mathematical formula to determine prices from the ratio of assets in the pool, rather than matching buyers and sellers in an order book.

Uniswap documentation and governance materials describe LPs as depositing tokens into pools so traders can swap token pairs through an on-chain AMM, with LPs generally earning a share of trading fees in return.

Uniswap v3 expanded the basic token-pair pool model by allowing concentrated liquidity and, in some cases, single-token deposits within custom price ranges, but the core AMM concept still centers on pooled on-chain liquidity used for swaps.

Programmatic token sales on exchanges no.canonical: Programmatic token sales on exchanges not necessarily securities transactions
Programmatic token sales on exchanges no.summary: In SEC v. Ripple Labs, Judge Analisa Torres’s July 13, 2023 summary-judgment ruling held that Ripple’s "programmatic sales" of XRP on digital-asset exchanges did not, on the specific facts presented, constitute investment contracts under Howey, even though Ripple’s direct institutional sales did. The court’s reasoning focused on the transactional context: exchange trades were blind bid/ask transactions, so buyers generally did not know they were purchasing from Ripple and therefore could not reasonably rely on Ripple’s efforts in the same way as institutional counterparties. The ruling is widely described as fact-specific and not a blanket holding that all secondary-market token sales are outside securities law.
keyPoints: The court distinguished between transaction types: institutional XRP sales were held to be unregistered securities offerings, while programmatic exchange sales were not, showing that the same token can be sold in ways that are or are not securities transactions depending on the facts.

A key factual basis was that programmatic buyers on exchanges typically did not know whether they were buying from Ripple or another seller because the trades occurred through blind bid/ask mechanisms.
The opinion emphasized that XRP itself was not inherently a security; instead, the court analyzed whether particular contracts, transactions, or schemes satisfied the Howey test.

The SEC challenged the programmatic-sales holding, but commentary after the decision noted that Judge Torres did not hold that all secondary-market sales of crypto tokens are categorically non-securities transactions; the analysis remained context-dependent.

SEC classification of most cryptocurrenc.canonical: SEC classification of most cryptocurrencies as securities
SEC classification of most cryptocurrenc.summary: Search results from SEC materials support a nuanced version of the concept: the SEC has often taken the position in enforcement and staff-level analysis that many crypto assets may be securities under the Howey investment-contract test, but it has not issued a blanket rule that "most cryptocurrencies" are securities. Across SEC-related filings and statements, Bitcoin and Ether are repeatedly identified as the main publicly noted exceptions, based largely on their decentralized networks and the SEC's historical reluctance to allege they are securities.
keyPoints: SEC analysis typically treats crypto-asset status as a facts-and-circumstances question under the Howey test, not as an automatic classification for all tokens.

Multiple SEC-related filings state that Bitcoin and Ethereum were the only specific cryptocurrencies that senior SEC officials had publicly said were unlikely to be securities, while some centrally issued assets received SEC staff no-action relief.

Recent SEC materials also clarify that a crypto asset is not necessarily itself a security in all contexts; rather, it can be sold as part of an investment contract depending on the surrounding offers, sales, and promotional scheme.

SEC-related 2025 materials and registrant disclosures still describe regulatory uncertainty: the SEC has historically not publicly asserted that Bitcoin or Ether are securities, but has brought actions involving many other tokens and offerings.

Bitcoin and Ethereum as exceptions to SE.canonical: Bitcoin and Ethereum as exceptions to SEC classification
Bitcoin and Ethereum as exceptions to SE.summary: SEC materials show that Bitcoin and Ether have often been treated as notable exceptions in crypto securities discussions, but the basis differs and Ethereum’s status has been less definitive. In a June 14, 2018 SEC speech, William Hinman said that, given Ether’s then-current decentralized structure, current offers and sales of Ether were not securities transactions, and he said the same disclosure logic added little value for Bitcoin. SEC guidance published in 2019 framed this around the Howey test: a digital asset may cease to involve a securities transaction if purchasers are no longer relying on a central group’s managerial efforts. However, later SEC-related materials and public reporting indicate Hinman’s speech was widely viewed as his own staff-level view rather than a formal Commission rule, so Bitcoin is the clearer exception while Ethereum has remained more contested.
keyPoints: In the SEC’s June 14, 2018 speech, William Hinman stated that 'current offers and sales of Ether are not securities transactions' and said that, as with Bitcoin, applying federal securities-law disclosure rules to current transactions would add little value.

The SEC’s 2019 digital-asset framework explains the principle behind that exception: under Howey, a token may fall outside securities treatment when the network is sufficiently decentralized and buyers are not relying on a promoter’s essential managerial efforts.

A 2019 SEC speech by Commissioner Hester Peirce described Hinman’s remarks as a 'well-known speech' and reiterated that digital assets can be securities if they meet Howey, implying the exception is transactional and fact-specific rather than a blanket exemption for crypto assets.

Senate Banking Committee materials from February 6, 2018 confirm Jay Clayton’s role in early SEC crypto oversight, while later SEC-related commentary and reporting show Ethereum’s classification remained less settled than Bitcoin’s, despite Hinman’s 2018 statement.

SEC v. Ripple case (2023).summary: In SEC v. Ripple Labs, Judge Analisa Torres’s July 13, 2023 summary-judgment ruling drew a transaction-by-transaction distinction under the Howey test rather than holding XRP itself to be a security. The court held that Ripple’s direct institutional XRP sales were unregistered securities offerings, but its "programmatic sales" on crypto trading platforms were not, because the SEC failed to show that an objective public-market buyer would reasonably expect profits from Ripple’s managerial efforts in those blind bid/ask transactions.
keyPoints: The ruling was issued on July 13, 2023, by the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York in SEC v. Ripple Labs, Inc.; it granted summary judgment in part to both sides.
The court said Ripple’s institutional sales of XRP violated Section 5 of the Securities Act, while programmatic sales on exchanges did not satisfy Howey’s expectation-of-profits-from-others prong.

In discussing programmatic sales, the court emphasized that exchange buyers generally could not know whether they were buying from Ripple, many may not have known Ripple’s role, and those sales represented less than 1% of global XRP trading volume.

The decision was influential but limited: later 2023 filings show the SEC sought interlocutory appeal on the programmatic-sales issue, and the court clarified that its ruling turned on the specific factual record and totality of circumstances in Ripple rather than a blanket rule for all secondary-market crypto sales.
